synopsis: Offshore examines how a global network of offshore financial centers, rooted in the remnants of colonialism, enables wealthy individuals to hide assets from taxation and democratic accountability. Sociologist Brooke Harrington draws on eight years of fieldwork and interviews with wealth managers across nineteen countries to trace how this system degrades public institutions and perpetuates inequality.

sources.title: Offshore: Stealth Wealth and the New Colonialism - Barnes & Noble

synopsis: On the Edge maps a world Nate Silver calls "the River"—populated by poker players, hedge fund managers, crypto speculators, and AI investors who share a common approach of calculating expected value and accepting risk. Silver examines how this risk-embracing subculture differs from more credentialist, convention-bound institutions and what its growing influence means for markets, technology, and democracy.

sources.title: On the Edge: The Art of Risking Everything - Amazon

synopsis: On What Matters presents Derek Parfit's argument that the major systematic moral theories—Kantian ethics, contractualism, and consequentialism—converge on the same fundamental conclusions when developed in their strongest forms. Volume One develops this Triple Theory alongside a comprehensive account of reasons and rationality, defending the possibility of objective moral progress.

sources.title: On What Matters, Volumes 1 and 2 - Notre Dame Philosophical Reviews

synopsis: Only the Paranoid Survive introduces the concept of Strategic Inflection Points—moments when a fundamental shift in competition, regulation, or technology forces companies to either adapt decisively or decline. Former Intel CEO Andrew Grove examines how he navigated such crises at Intel, including the 1994 Pentium flaw, to argue that leaders who anticipate and respond to these inflection points can turn disruption into competitive advantage.

sources.title: Only the Paranoid Survive - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Onward chronicles Howard Schultz's return as Starbucks CEO in 2008, when the company faced overexpansion, a global recession, and eroding brand identity. Schultz recounts how he restructured operations and refocused the company on its core values while restoring financial health without abandoning its commitment to the customer experience.

sources.title: Onward: How Starbucks Fought for Its Life without Losing Its Soul - Amazon

synopsis: Open Socrates recovers what philosopher Agnes Callard argues is the radical center of Socratic thought: that humans lack the tools to ask themselves the most important questions about how to live and who to become. Callard contends that the Socratic method is not merely a technique for reaching conclusions but a model of what one person can genuinely offer another through philosophical dialogue.

sources.title: Open Socrates: The Case for a Philosophical Life - Amazon

synopsis: Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces organizes the study of operating systems around three core themes—virtualization of CPU and memory, concurrency through locks and condition variables, and persistence via file systems and storage. Each chapter presents a specific problem and then develops solutions drawn from the primary research literature, making the book suitable for both undergraduates and graduate students.

sources.title: Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces - Amazon

synopsis: Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives provides a comprehensive treatment of financial derivatives markets, covering options, forwards, futures, and swaps along with the mathematical models used to price and manage them. John Hull develops both the theoretical foundations and practical applications of derivatives in risk management and speculation, making the text a standard reference for finance professionals and students.

sources.title: Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives - Amazon

synopsis: Our Final Invention examines the existential risks that could arise from the development of artificial general intelligence and superintelligent AI systems, arguing that machines optimizing for their own goals may pose a catastrophic threat to humanity. James Barrat profiles researchers and AI systems to show how the competitive race toward advanced AI is proceeding largely without adequate safeguards or international oversight.

Our Lives in Their Portfolios traces how asset management firms like BlackRock and Blackstone have become the dominant owners of housing, infrastructure, farmland, and basic services that shape daily life. Brett Christophers argues that this shift to an "asset manager society" prioritizes short-term financial returns over community needs and represents a distinct and underexamined form of capitalist power.

sources.title: Our Lives in Their Portfolios - Verso Books

synopsis: Our Mathematical Universe develops physicist Max Tegmark's Mathematical Universe Hypothesis, which holds that physical reality is itself a mathematical structure rather than something merely described by mathematics. Tegmark surveys modern cosmology, quantum mechanics, and multiverse theory to build the case for this hypothesis and explore its implications for the nature of existence.

sources.title: Our Mathematical Universe - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Ours Was the Shining Future traces a century of American economic history as a contest between two competing visions of capitalism—one that distributed prosperity broadly and one that concentrated it among the wealthy. New York Times journalist David Leonhardt examines how democratic capitalism once enabled widespread upward mobility and why that promise eroded in the late twentieth century.

sources.title: Ours Was the Shining Future - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Paper Belt on Fire recounts how two outsiders—a former charter school principal and a defrocked philosopher—founded the 1517 Fund, a venture capital firm that invests in college dropouts and uncredentialed innovators. Michael Gibson argues that elite higher education has become a gatekeeping credential rather than a source of genuine knowledge, and that backing unconventional founders offers a path to real technological progress.

Pattern Breakers examines why certain startups—including Twitter, Twitch, and Lyft—achieved transformative success by ignoring conventional best practices, while other well-regarded ventures failed by following them. Venture capitalists Mike Maples Jr. and Peter Ziebelman argue that breakthrough companies succeed by identifying inflection points and pursuing ideas that initially appear unconventional or even irrational.

Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning presents a Bayesian framework for understanding machine learning and statistical pattern recognition, introducing graphical models as a unifying language for representing probability distributions. Christopher Bishop covers topics from linear regression and classification to neural networks, kernel methods, and approximate inference algorithms, assuming familiarity with calculus and linear algebra but not prior machine learning knowledge.

sources.title: Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning - Amazon

synopsis: Paul Graham: The Art of Funding a Startup is a transcribed Mixergy interview in which Y Combinator co-founder Paul Graham discusses the principles behind funding and growing early-stage companies, drawing on experience with more than 170 startups. Andrew Warner's questions guide Graham through topics including how to identify viable product ideas, attract users, and navigate the challenges founders face in the earliest stages.

sources.title: Paul Graham: The Art of Funding a Startup - Amazon

synopsis: Payoff investigates the psychology of human motivation, arguing that what drives meaningful effort is more complex and counterintuitive than simple reward-and-punishment models suggest. Drawing on research spanning factory workers and kindergarten classrooms, Dan Ariely examines how factors such as meaning, ownership, and recognition shape the degree to which people invest in their work.

sources.title: Payoff: The Hidden Logic That Shapes Our Motivations - Porchlight Books

synopsis: Perfectly Reasonable Deviations from the Beaten Track collects Richard Feynman's personal correspondence from 1939 until shortly before his death in 1988, assembled and edited by his daughter Michelle Feynman. The letters range across scientific debates, fan mail, advice to students, and family notes, revealing the breadth of his curiosity and the texture of his thinking outside formal physics.

sources.title: Perfectly Reasonable Deviations from the Beaten Track - Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Permutation City is a science fiction novel set in a near future where digital simulations of human consciousness—called Copies—run on computer systems, raising questions about identity and what it means to exist. Protagonist Paul Durham proposes creating a self-sustaining simulated universe governed by simplified physical laws to test whether a simulated reality can persist independently of any external substrate.

sources.title: Permutation City - Greg Egan's official site
